speaker: interviewer
text: --December 21, 1995, Ira Kaminsky. My name is Dan Sedlis, tape number 2. So you said your sister made Aliyah to Palestine in 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you gave-- you gave thought to doing the same? You, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely. Well-- and I couldn't do it. Times were bad even. And my friends went and not only illegal-- with the illegal Aliyah, we could go-- that our party organized. Some of my friends succeeded. And I wanted to go. But just before the war, they went to Romania. And England had their spies watching the illegal Aliyahs-- immigrations. And they intervened with the, the Romanian government that they should stop the illegal Aliyah to then Palestine. And I, I just missed it. I didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this prevented you from going--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted very badly-- let it be, for the records.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say the young people-- the majority of the young people were very, very much interested to go either way because legally, England was not interested to let the Jewish people immigrate that time. But they would-- the only way was at that time illegally, you know, through Aliyah Bet, we used to call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that before '39, you taught in Lodz in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you came back from Lodz--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in Chmielnik at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I was already-- I was working with my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- we supported ourselves, you know. It was very, very bad, those days economically. Times were bad at that time. And my brother-- as a matter of fact, my brother tried also to go to a kibbutz--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and go to Israel, follow my sister's--

speaker: interviewee
text: --my brother Harry. And he came back. And meantime, they took him to the Polish Army. And this was in 1938, in the beginning of 1938. As a matter of fact, he was in the Polish Army for a year and a half. When he was supposed to be released, World War II started. And they kept him in the army also. And he was that time at war with the Nazis, in the Polish Army. As you know, they crushed the Polish Army within two weeks. And finally, thank God, he came home. And he stayed with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were living in Chmielnik at the time, September 1st. And your entire family was there, except for Hannah, who was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened on September 1st? How did it affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't realize the Nazis invaded Poland. And they invaded also our city, Chmielnik. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they showed a ugly face immediately at the first night when they came into our city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They collected-- they went through-- they had their collaborators. They went through, and they collected about 12 or 15 of the most prominent Jewish people in Chmielnik. And they took them to the synagogue, the [NON-ENGLISH]. They closed it. And they set the [NON-ENGLISH] on fire. Some people ran out. They were shooting after them. And this was the first time they showed their, their real face.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that there were collaborators. These were Polish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names, any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember their names. But they had--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said they took the four most prominent Jews. And, and what happened to the Jews? They were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them were killed. And some of them escaped from the burning synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of these Jews or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was a-- the chairman-- a chairman of the, the Jewish community at that time, [NON-ENGLISH] Selman. He, he was a-- he had a restaurant over there, not far, next to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they survive, [NON-ENGLISH] and Selman, at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], yes. One was-- one was wounded-- the ear, the neck. They shot his head. And Selman survived. [NON-ENGLISH] survived. And some of them didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was the first thing. The second-- the second day, I had-- personally, I had a bad experience. The second day, they came to our house. Why did they come to our house was three roads came together to one road, which lead-- it was leading to Kielce. And we lived right there in the beginning of that, that, that street. And they were-- and they were interested to keep it safe. They came in. And they took us out, my family. And I was the only young man over there. And I was-- at that time, I was 20 years old. My father, kids, and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was before Herschl came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was before Herschl came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: --when the Nazis invaded our city. And I was the only one. And they saw a young man. They accused me that I was hiding [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] ammunition. And they started beating me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was over there, everybody, my father. They suffered that time. Where are-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], they call it. Where did you-- where, where, where did you hide this-- the, the ammunition? And they were hitting me terribly. And I said, I don't have any [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after they, they beat me up very badly, they said that all families should leave that house. And they said, you should-- don't you ever come back. And we had to leave our house. And we felt on our own [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] body what the real Nazis are. And this wasn't even the SS. This was the Wehrmact, the soldiers only.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, we had to do--

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? We went to-- not far. Over there, we had-- my mother had some sisters over there, three sisters. She went to one sister. She lived over there in the same building, where-- it used to be a school. And all the schools were closed at that time. And we said, let's go over there because we have-- maybe we can find a place where to stay there. And we stayed there for a while, until it cleared up, until the army left. And they went all the way further. And they captured Poland. And then we went back to our house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A week later, something like it, Herschl came back from the army in very bad shape. He had to change his clothes. Otherwise, they would take him, the Nazis. He changed. He took off his-- this-- the, the army clothes. And he got somewhere some civilian clothes. And he came home. He was, of course-- he traveled by foot. And he came home. And we were very, very happy. And after a while,   he came to himself. My mother took good care of him. And he survived the war, that time. And since then, it started, the gendarmerie--

speaker: interviewee
text: --gendarmes came to our city. They created-- the first thing is, they created a council, a Jewish council. And all the orders, they gave it to them. And they-- and they were responsible they should be filled out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Number one, they put on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who was on the council? It's the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That time-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that time was one Zalczman. Zalczman was. Then there was another one later on. They killed him--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very dedicated-- a very dedicated Jewish man, formal, a very active man in the Zionist movement. And they were asking him for the impossible. Number one, contributions, contributions, terrible amount-- great amounts of contributions that we couldn't even get it from the people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the contribution that we had to pay to the Germans. Then it came-- they wanted to know who is a Jew-- they were-- they were interested-- separate-- separate the Polish people and the Jewish people. Then it came a, a order to wear the arm band, the Star of David. And for not wearing the arm band, the Star of David-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was a death sentence for not wearing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know anyone who was killed for not wearing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they were-- somebody-- there was some of them that traveled. They took a chance. They traveled. And they arrested them. And we didn't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were deported. You never saw them again.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know-- we never saw them again. Some, some of them, they were killed just right over there. And, you know-- and then it started-- this was close to the winter. Every day, you know, the snow-- to remove the snow and clear the roads for the Nazis-- the Jewish people had to go. They didn't even supply the shovels. We had to supply our own and clear-- clean this-- the, the roads for them--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Let me understand something. When the gendarmerie came, and you started to wear the yellow--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have the yellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wore the white and blue Star of David. And were you still living in your home--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was-- I was at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a Jewish council, but there was no ghetto yet at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no ghetto yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on-- there, there were-- number one, they, they, they want to know who is a Jew to the third generation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody was-- you have-- you had to sign it and be responsible. There was nobody-- and they were watching if there was somebody who didn't sign it. And also death sentence for, for, for, for lying and for not doing it. And it, it started work force to create [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whatever they needed. They told this-- the, the council, which they called the 'Judenrat.'

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat. And they were responsible for supplying the labor force. I was that time assigned to work in the quarries to, to break stones, chop stones, for repair of, of the roads. And I was working for some times over there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was also without, without any pay, without any support, without any-- given any clothes, and everything. And this was in Chmielnik, in our own city. Later on, food was very scarce. It became very scarce, food. It became very, very expensive. Before the farmers, they used to come and sell it before the war. Now, at, at the war time, they didn't-- they were not allowed even to come to the city and sell anything to the Jewish people. And everything, if you want-- if you were interested to buy, you had to buy for some reason on the black market for exorbitant prices. They were supplied by the farmers food, but only on the black market. We were supposed to get some bread, some-- for everybody, a certain portion of bread. But they were-- they closed down all the-- all the bakeries. And they were open only two bakeries. And they didn't even supply what they were supposed to supply for the few people. Only on the black market-- in order to survive-- we had to buy. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any source of income that you had at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very, very bad because you were not-- no business was, was allowed, you know. My father was somehow, somehow also was interested to buy and sell from the-- from the farmers and sell it and sell it. And that's how he-- somehow, he made some kind of-- some kind of profit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he still have his trucks and his car?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. His cars, they took away everything. They wouldn't allow any Jewish men to, to own a car or a truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was any other property taken away from you yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- at that-- at that time, taxi was, was, was also taken away. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you still had the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: The apartment, we were lucky. We had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or the, the home. It was a home, actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our home, yes, it was our home. It was-- we, we had our home. We had two tenants. But later on, my sister got married in 1941. And she occupied the other two-- the other apartment--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the same house. --sister, --sister, Frida. My sister, Frida, married a husband, Kwashnieski. And she occupied the-- with us the other apartment, which, which was rented before. She-- later on, she had a child by the name of Baruch. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Baruch went. She lost him by the, the Nazis. They took him away. They took away the baby. The baby was only six weeks old.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the-- when was the baby taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: But before that, in 1941-- before the winter, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they called for 100 people-- 100 young men to go to a labor camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was among them. We had to go. If they called, we had to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you were called for Kielce Wisniowka, were you still living at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My home. And I went. And after a while, I saw it was terrible over there. The Ukrainian [GERMAN] were in charge. And the beating--

speaker: interviewee
text: They called them. It's a German word, like police.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] The beatings didn't stop. And I thought to myself, this is not the way to be. And this is not for me. And I started to investigate and find a way to get out. And after a couple of weeks being over there, I decided I have to go. I took with me a good-- a good friend of mine and another neighbor of mine, also who got a terrible beating, terrible beating. And I couldn't see the way he suffered.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked him, you want to stay? Or you want to go with me? I decided to go. OK, that's it. After a beating like this, my friend, he went with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we decided-- you know, I did something which nobody did before. Everybody-- there were people that they escaped from that camp. And they escaped always at night. At night, they thought it was safe to go. But the problem was, I noticed that they are-- the work-- the, the Ukrainians had dogs also. And at night, the dogs felt. If somebody-- if somebody moved around the camp, they, they caught them. And what I decided, it was-- people didn't even believe that I will succeed something like that. 12 o'clock, during the day, when everybody went for lunch, including the [GERMAN], I observed them for several days. And I saw that all of them went in 12 o'clock. And nobody was watching the camp. They had lunch. All of them went in to have lunch. That was the time we escaped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went. We were running. We weren't sure if they will-- if they will run after us. And we went to-- as a matter of fact, we found the first-- we went into the woods, through, through the forest. And we saw a few farmers were living over there. And I saw a little-- a young man coming out, about my age. And I told my man-- the two men with me, I'll go in. And you'll stay behind. You stay one side, and the other one, another side. I'll go in. I'll talk to him because I want to know even where I am in the nearest town where I-- where we should go. I will talk to you. I'll make signs to you because I am interested also that he should know that I am not alone. Because I didn't know if this was-- you know, among the Polish people, I'm sorry to say, there were collaborators. Or if they squealed on a Jew, they got a kilo of sugar or a bottle of vodka. Anyway, I'll go in, and I'll talk to him. If you'll see that-- some danger, then it's him or us. And everybody had a knife with them, including me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and I asked him, where-- I want to know, what's the name of this town, of this place that you are? He told me it wasn't even a-- it was a very small place. But the nearest was Bodzentyn, Bodzentyn. How far is it? About 10, 10-- it was what, 10 kilometers, not miles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said-- he said, you know, if you want to go with me, in a half hour, I'll go with you. Telephones he didn't have, you know, because there, he could have notified us to the police or, or this the [GERMAN] that I am over there. But he didn't. And he was nice enough. He went to the town also. And I went with him. But the other two were behind me, watching me, and watching both of us. When we came a couple of miles, when I saw already the town, I told him, thank you very much. I'm going a different way. I didn't want to tell him that I am going over there because he went there. And he could notify the police. And we rented-- and we-- we rented some kind of wagon. And we went home. And this was the end of that plan. We succeeded very well to escape. We were very happy [DRINKS] that we escaped from that-- from that camp. And some people died from, from hunger over there in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was with you at Kielce Wisniowka?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were from other towns also. But we were about a hundred from, from, from--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew them, many of them. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sure. I, I know them. [? Friedman-- ?] [? Friedman ?] is still alive, that I went-- I took him with me. The other guy, [NON-ENGLISH], he didn't survive. He went to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- I'm glad we survived that time. We succeeded with the escape. And later on, they were calling for 300 people. They said that they need 300 people. And they said, if you're going to go to this, you're going to be sure that nothing will happen, because that time, they were talking already, you know, that-- with the killings and all the other things. And people went there for 300 young men in about 10--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dobleze. It was-- it was about 20 or 30 kilometers from our city. To cut-- they call it turf for heating.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were-- they had-- they were working also in the forest to chop wood--

speaker: interviewer
text: Peat? They, they were cutting peat?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they weren't too long over there. And once they took them all out, and they killed them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find out about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know-- there were about a couple of, of woman-- about-- I don't know exactly, three or four women who were working in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those who were working in the kitchen, they wouldn't-- they didn't kill.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they saw what happened to the others, they escaped. The women ran away. And that's how-- that's how people-- we found out what happened to the 300 people in Dobleze.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Dobleze?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the people who were arranging--

speaker: interviewee
text: Send the young men was the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a matter of fact, my brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Harry was among them. And the last minute, our good friend, who was also active in, in the Judenrat, Yosl Kleinert, he survived. He took him out. And thanks to him, my brother survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Judenrat may have known at this point that there may have been killings that would take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they, they kept them until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when-- until before-- they killed them just at the same time as we-- when they liquidated Chmielnik.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this, the liquidation of Chmielnik?

speaker: interviewee
text: The liquidation of-- well, before liquidation, I would like to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: They organized-- they were calling everyone from, from 16 to 40, men and women, to come to the marketplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's-- there will be a selection for, for-- to send-- they didn't say where and when. Everybody has to report to the marketplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Judenrat made this announcement?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Judenrat made this announcement, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --understand something, that at this point in Chmielnik, there still was the same kind of an open ghetto where you wore the blue--

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there, it was an open ghetto. It wasn't a closed ghetto like in Lodz--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because most of the people were, were Jewish. And they were all over the city. What we had to do is to close the front entrances and close-- border up all the windows, all the doors, not to go in or out through the main street--

speaker: interviewee
text: --where it was facing, the window or the doors.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to go out through the back street.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remained in your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- yes, we remained in our home, only through the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the main street, a Jew was not allowed to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was the creation of the ghetto before--

speaker: interviewee
text: This creation of the ghetto was in, in April of 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the center became not the main streets, but the side streets--

speaker: interviewer
text: --stayed in their same homes. The, the-- you--

speaker: interviewee
text: People stayed the same home. But the problem was, it became so crowded.

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought in from the-- around-- from the neighboring, little towns, they brought in to Chmielnik. And from a big city, Plock--

speaker: interviewee
text: --they brought in about a thousand people from Plock.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were already crowded very badly. But we had to find and squeeze and locate all the thousand people from Plock.
